metadata.title: Claude, Redrawn.
metadata.description: diagram-redraw skill teardown: mine Wikipedia media refs for editorial usefulness signal, classify (doable/logo/photo), redraw concept figures in house style.

beats.narration_text: Olá, Liam. Today: diagram-redraw — turning Wikipedia's editorial judgment into house-style figures. Mine a topic's media references, classify the doable ones, redraw them fresh. Liam, in for Bear.

props.runningText: * reading facts/ → classify → rank → select → redraw in house style

beats.narration_text: Three inputs. facts-slash-topic-slash-terms.json — media references, one per Wikipedia figure. facts-slash-topic-slash-graph.json — concept link graph, in-degree says how foundational each concept is. Optional target book — chapters and house style filter for relevance. Three outputs: candidate plan, redrawn SVG figures, and a log.

whatItDoes: Mine facts/<topic>/ Wikipedia media refs for editorial usefulness signal
Classify: doable diagrams / logos (skip) / photos (skip) / other
Rank by foundational-ness (graph in_degree); float book-relevant concepts
Redraw selected figures fresh in house style (brutalist/DESIGN.md + cajal)
phaseGates: Stage 1: classify + rank (harvest.mjs — deterministic, no AI)
Stage 2: human selection (curate candidate plan; drop non-useful)
Stage 3: redraw in house style (AI, parallelizable per concept)

beats.narration_text: Three stages. Stage one: classify and rank — deterministic, no AI, runs node harvest.mjs. Every media ref gets a bucket: doable, logo, photo, or other. Stage two: human selection — read the candidate plan, keep what the book needs, drop the rest. Stage three: redraw — one SVG per concept, house style, AI-assisted but human-gated.

steps.label: Stage 2: Select
steps.detail: human curates candidate plan; drop logos, photos, irrelevant

steps.label: Stage 3: Redraw
steps.detail: one SVG per concept, fresh — brutalist/DESIGN.md + cajal style

steps.label: Log
steps.detail: what was drawn + what each figure references

beats.narration_text: A Wikipedia page on quantum mechanics has forty-seven images. Doable diagrams, trademarked logos, copyrighted photographs. How many are actually redrawable? The classification split decides where the work is.

props.runningText: * scanning 47 media refs → classify each → rank doable set

beats.narration_text: Self-demo: Stage 1 candidate plan for quantum mechanics — free, deterministic. Harvest.mjs scans the topic, classifies all forty-seven media refs, ranks the nineteen doable figures by foundational-ness. Top candidate: wave-function diagram, in-degree ninety-four. The candidate plan is the free output — no SVG is drawn until the human selects from this list.

codeLines: # SELF-DEMO: Stage 1 candidate plan (deterministic — no AI)
# OUTPUT (no SVG drawn until human selects):
captionLines: Stage 1 is free and deterministic. No AI call, no SVG drawn.
Human curates this list before Stage 3 (redraw) begins.

beats.narration_text: Law one: every image on a Wikipedia page is there because a human editor decided it earned its place — that editorial judgment is a usefulness signal we can harvest. We're not scraping images. We're borrowing the curation decision. The editor chose to include a wave-function diagram because it teaches something. That's the signal worth keeping.

props.quote: Every image on a Wikipedia page is there because a human editor decided it earned its place. That editorial judgment is a usefulness signal we can harvest.
mechanismLines: Wikipedia editorial judgment = figure usefulness signal
We borrow the curation decision, not the image expression
The concept is free; the drawing is copyrighted
in_degree amplifies: foundational concepts float to the top

beats.narration_text: Law two: never copy a source image — redraw the idea in house style. Don't download or embed the Commons file. The Commons image is a reference for what the concept looks like. The drawing is ours. A Bohr-model diagram redrawn in cream and terracotta is a new work expressing a standard idea — that's legally and pedagogically clean.

props.quote: Never copy a source image. Redraw the idea in house style. Don't download/embed the Commons file.
mechanismLines: Use Commons image as concept reference only — never trace or embed
Redraw from the idea (the standard concept); the drawing is ours
Cite the concept, not the picture — never imply it's the Wikipedia image

beats.narration_text: Law three: useful equals the book needs it. The editorial signal gets a concept onto the candidate list — relevance to the target book decides whether it's drawn. A wave-function diagram is foundational in quantum mechanics, but if the book chapter doesn't cover it, it doesn't get drawn. The greyscale-first discipline applies: a figure with no job doesn't get a canvas.

props.quote: Useful = the book needs it. The editorial signal gets a concept onto the candidate list; relevance to the target book decides whether it's drawn.
mechanismLines: Editorial signal → candidate list; book relevance → drawn list
Drop anything already covered by an existing figure
Greyscale-first: a figure with no job doesn't get drawn
--book flag floats relevant concepts above foundational-only ranking

beats.narration_text: Claude, Redrawn. The artifact is a fresh SVG — not the Wikipedia image, not a trace, not a download. A wave-function diagram in house style: cream canvas, ink, EB Garamond. The editorial signal found the concept. The book decided it was needed. The skill drew it clean.

deliveryLines: Editorial signal → candidate list → human selection
Never copy · redraw the idea · cite the concept
Useful = the book needs it · greyscale-first

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Pick a topic in your facts folder. Run Stage 1 — it's free and deterministic. Curate the candidate list. Then draw. Liam, handing off to Bear.

output: step 3: redraw selected figures in house style

beats.narration_text: Claude, Redrawn. At NikBearBrown. Liam, in for Bear.
